rationale: Both mentions refer to the gradient of a scalar-valued function, a fundamental concept in multivariable calculus. Evidence_001 defines the gradient as a vector of partial derivatives, and evidence_008 expresses the gradient of a composite function using the chain rule. The concept is identical across lectures.

evidence_spans.span: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}\) be a differentiable scalar-valued function. A partial derivative measures how \(f\) changes when one coordinate changes while the other coordinates are held fixed. The gradient \(\nabla f(x)\) collects these partial derivatives into a vector. For a unit direction \(u\), the directional derivative is \(\nabla f(x)\cdot u\), so the gradient points in the direction of steepest local increase. A point where the gradient is the zero vector is called a stationary point.
